title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
text:
0.1 Why this tutorial exists
Biodiversity data is only useful to decision-makers if it can be found, trusted, and compared across studies. That is the problem Essential Biodiversity Variables (EBVs) were designed to solve: a common language and file format so that a species-distribution model from one team and a community-composition model from another can sit side by side on the same map.
This tutorial walks you through the full path from “I have some raster outputs on my laptop” to “my dataset is live on the EBV Data Portal” — using a real, published dataset as the running example, so every step produces something you can check against the real thing.
By the end, you will be able to:
crumbs: 1  From Raster to Portal: Publishing Biodiversity Data as an EBV NetCDF

title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
crumbs: 1  From Raster to Portal: Publishing Biodiversity Data as an EBV NetCDF

title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
section: 0.2 What is an EBV, in one paragraph
text:
0.2 What is an EBV, in one paragraph
An Essential Biodiversity Variable is a measurement standardized well enough to be tracked consistently over time, across regions, and across research groups — the biodiversity equivalent of an Essential Climate Variable. GEO BON groups them into six classes (genetic composition, species populations, species traits, community composition, ecosystem structure, ecosystem function), and every EBV dataset shares the same underlying file format: a CF- and ACDD-compliant netCDF with up to four dimensions — longitude, latitude, time, and entity (species, group, or ecosystem type). That shared format is what lets the EBV Data Portal host, filter, and compare hundreds of otherwise very different datasets.
crumbs: 1  From Raster to Portal: Publishing Biodiversity Data as an EBV NetCDF

title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
text:
0.3 The dataset we’ll use
Rather than a toy example, this tutorial anchors its portal-facing sections to a real, already-published dataset:

Global trends in biodiversity (BES-SIM cSAR-iDiv) — Inês Martins & Henrique Pereira (2022). Community-composition projections from 1900–2050 under multiple SSP/RCP scenarios, produced for the BES-SIM inter-model comparison for IPBES. Dataset page · DOI: 10.25829/5zmy41 · netCDF download: 3.27 MB

We use this dataset (portal ID 30) whenever we’re exploring the portal — its metadata form, entity list, DOI, citation, and versioning — so everything you see corresponds to something you can open yourself, right now, on the live portal.
For the hands-on R code, we mostly use the small example file bundled with the ebvcube package (martins_comcom_subset.nc), so every chunk runs instantly with no download. That file is a related but different dataset by the same author and model family — portal ID 1, “Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM)”, a spatial (African) subset of a 1900–2015 land-use-driven bird diversity product. Unit 3 shows how to download the actual dataset‑30 netCDF (small enough, at 3.27 MB, to work with directly) if you want to run the full workflow against it end-to-end instead.
crumbs: 1  From Raster to Portal: Publishing Biodiversity Data as an EBV NetCDF

title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
text:
0.4 The workflow at a glance

flowchart LR
    A["1. Prepare your data\n(rasters + metadata)"] --> B["2. Generate the metadata JSON\n(portal form or ebv_metadata())"]
    B --> C["3. ebv_create()\nempty, valid netCDF skeleton"]
    C --> D["4. ebv_add_data()\nfill in each entity / time step"]
    D --> E["5. Validate\nebv_properties(), Panoply, portal validator"]
    E --> F["6. Upload to the\nEBV Data Portal"]
    F --> G["7. Review & publish\n(DOI assigned)"]




flowchart LR
    A["1. Prepare your data\n(rasters + metadata)"] --> B["2. Generate the metadata JSON\n(portal form or ebv_metadata())"]
    B --> C["3. ebv_create()\nempty, valid netCDF skeleton"]
    C --> D["4. ebv_add_data()\nfill in each entity / time step"]
    D --> E["5. Validate\nebv_properties(), Panoply, portal validator"]
    E --> F["6. Upload to the\nEBV Data Portal"]
    F --> G["7. Review & publish\n(DOI assigned)"]

title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
text:
0.5 How this tutorial is organized



Unit
Topic




1
Introduction to EBVs and the EBV Data Portal


2
Installing R, RStudio, and the ebvcube package


3
Preparing your biodiversity data for conversion


4
Creating an EBV netCDF with ebvcube


5
Validating the generated file


6
Uploading your dataset to the EBV Data Portal


7
Troubleshooting common issues and best practices


8
Further resources, repositories, and references



Each unit opens with its learning objectives, works through the step with runnable code and screenshots, and closes with a short checkpoint so you know you’re ready to move on.

title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
crumbs: 1  From Raster to Portal: Publishing Biodiversity Data as an EBV NetCDF

title: Creating an EBV NetCDF and Publishing to the EBV Data Portal
text:
0.7 Key resources

EBV Data Portal — https://portal.geobon.org/ — discover, download, and publish EBV datasets.
ebvcube R package — https://github.com/EBVcube/ebvcube — read, visualize, and create EBV netCDFs. Also on CRAN and the b-cubed r-universe.
ebvcube documentation book — https://EBVcube.github.io/ebvcube/ — the full function reference this tutorial builds on.
EBV netCDF format specification — https://github.com/EBVcube/EBVCube-format — the structural rules behind the format.
GEO BON — What are EBVs? — https://geobon.org/ebvs/what-are-ebvs/ — the canonical definition and the six EBV classes.
Methods paper — Quoss et al. (2022), describing the package and the netCDF structure it implements.







Working alongside this tutorial. Every code chunk is runnable as-is against the bundled example dataset. If you’re preparing your own dataset for the portal, follow the same steps but substitute your rasters and metadata where indicated in Units 3–4.



Ready? Start with Unit 1 — Introduction to EBVs and the EBV Data Portal.
crumbs: 1  From Raster to Portal: Publishing Biodiversity Data as an EBV NetCDF

title: 2  Introduction to EBVs and the EBV Data Portal
text:
2.4 Touring the EBV Data Portal
We’ll use dataset 30, “Global trends in biodiversity (BES-SIM cSAR-iDiv)” by Inês Martins & Henrique Pereira, as our worked example throughout this tutorial.







A note on datasets



This tutorial uses two related datasets by the same research group:

Dataset 30 (above) — our real-world example whenever we talk about the portal itself.
The small file bundled with the ebvcube package — a subset of a different dataset (portal ID 1, “Local bird diversity”) — which we use for hands-on R code so nothing needs downloading.

Unit 3 shows how to download dataset 30’s actual file if you want full end-to-end fidelity.

title: 9  Additional resources, documentation, repositories, and references
text:
ebvcube R package — https://github.com/EBVcube/ebvcube, CRAN, b-cubed r-universe
ebvcube documentation book — https://EBVcube.github.io/ebvcube/
EBV netCDF format specification — https://github.com/EBVcube/EBVCube-format
EBV Data Portal — https://portal.geobon.org/
GEO BON — What are EBVs? — https://geobon.org/ebvs/what-are-ebvs/
Example dataset (portal) — Martins, I., Pereira, H. (2022). Global trends in biodiversity (BES-SIM cSAR-iDiv) (Version 1) [Dataset]. iDiv. https://doi.org/10.25829/5zmy41
Getting help — GitHub issues, GitHub Discussions, portal contact
